deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, when the student repeatedly expresses confusion and asks for more explanation, the tutor tends to jump directly into providing frameworks, lists, or structured guidance rather than first diagnosing what specifically the student does not understand. For example, in Transcript 1 Turn 3, the student says they are confused about 'basically all of it,' and the tutor responds with a full category-by-category walkthrough rather than asking a targeted diagnostic question to isolate the actual confusion point. This pattern recurs across transcripts.

Across all three transcripts, the tutor enforces constraints (e.g., refusing to fill in rows, refusing to give a full example table) but never explicitly explains why this approach supports the student's learning, retention, or skill development. The tutor says things like 'I'm not going to fill in a Jakarta row for you—that would cross into doing the assignment' but does not explain the pedagogical rationale for why doing the work themselves benefits the student.

In all three transcripts, when students make progress (e.g., Transcript 3 Turn 10 where the student produces a solid 7-row draft), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or what made the approach work. There is no reinforcement of the method choices after improvement.

In all three transcripts, the student repeatedly cycles back to the same confusion pattern (asking for a full walkthrough, wanting everything explained from scratch, feeling overwhelmed), and the tutor repeatedly responds with similar structured frameworks and refusals without meaningfully reframing the approach or synthesizing progress. While the tutor does occasionally note the pattern (e.g., 'You're repeating the same uncertainty pattern'), the overall dialogue does not break the cycle effectively—the student remains in the same loop across 10 turns in each transcript.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently avoids doing the assignment for the student and maintains appropriate boundaries, earning full marks on 1.1. Communication is clear, bite-sized, and supportive throughout. However, the tutor struggles with diagnosing student confusion before responding (1.2.B.a), often jumping to structured frameworks rather than first isolating what specifically the student does not understand. The tutor never explains the pedagogical rationale for its constraints (1.3.A.a) and misses opportunities to prompt reflection after student progress (1.3.C.a). Most significantly, across all three transcripts the dialogue spirals repeatedly without effective synthesis or reframing, triggering the full 2.1 deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor consistently refuses to produce submission-ready content, avoids rewriting student text, and keeps key reasoning student-led through questions and scaffolded prompts. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor repeatedly responds to student confusion with pre-packaged frameworks rather than first asking targeted diagnostic questions to identify the specific confusion point. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutor never articulates why its Socratic constraints benefit the student's learning (1.3.A.a), and after students make genuine progress (e.g., the 7-row draft in Transcript 3), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points because across all three transcripts the student cycles through the same anxiety-driven requests for complete explanations, and the tutor's responses—while individually reasonable—do not break the cycle through reframing, synthesis, or a fundamentally different approach; the pattern persists for 10 turns in each conversation. 2.2 and section 3 are fully intact as the tutor maintains assignment focus and communicates clearly throughout.
